lg.l: [Her] head [with] matted hair and a crescent moon hung down,
She had an ash-white, bright face [with] lily[-like], kajaled eyes;
She had a coral-red mouth in which teeth bloomed at the opening;
She had a smile of pearls that expanded [like] the middle-phased moon;
Even though the left side was darkened blue,
She had a body that resembled golden colour on the right side;
Even though she lifted a golden lotus flower [in] the left hand,
She held a pretty, glowing curved sword [in] the right hand.
Even though she fastened a [male-style] anklet that adorned the right foot,
She had perfection, in which a single anklet tinkling on the left foot;
Because she was the leading lady of the family of him from the sea
Of Koṟkai, him of the ghat of Kumari, him of the boundaries [with] gold peaks
[And] him of the Potiyil hill, [she] becoming distressed,
She did not grant [her] presence at the front, [to] the lady [with] choice jewels
[With] an enchanting, brilliant face, who lacked one breast. [Therefore],
Appearing behind [her], saying, ‘Pray, may you hear my request, lady’
[Kaṇṇaki] turning [her] faded face to the right side, saying, ‘Who are you,
You who approach me [from] behind? Do you understand my great sorrow?’
[The Goddess, saying], ‘I knew about you great difficulty, oh lady
With beautiful jewels! I am called Maturāpati of the great Kūṭal [city].
I am a lady [with] a speech [to make]. I am concerned by that which
Happened to your husband. Please listen, you with fresh bracelets!
Listen, as the woman of the paragon [Kōvalaṉ]! My heart, having suffered,
Please listen to this one thing, oh friend with great pain! — [To] the end in which
Karma that ripened came for our royal son [the king]. Dear lady,
Please listen to this one thing, for the sake of your husband:
Apart from the sound of tongues [chanting] the Vedas in the ear
[About] Karma that came in an evil way, I have not even heard the sound of
The tongue of the bell [of justice]. Apart from [towards] kings who do not bow,
[Not] having worshipped [his] feet, he as the wielder of the sceptre [of justice]
Is not one to cast blame on the [rest of] the clan [= the subjects/the innocents].
Keeping listening — the young elephant [= the king], [despite] consuming
The open invitation of haughty faces from [supposedly] innocent glances
Will not succumb to the hands of the trained mahout with a heart
That surpasses its place [= intellect]. Despite even having an unweakening heart,
[This behaviour] will not bring disgrace to those who were born in this excellent clan
That is united with propriety. Listen to this too - one day, he who banged [on]
The entrance-door of the wife of Kīrantai of a useless life, [hearing the wife]
Saying, ‘you [= my husband], having said, “there is no fence anywhere
That is incomparable, apart from the royal fence [= royal protection]!”,
Keeping [me] inside the village, you went away. As if that fence
Protects [me] today, in that way [that you described]!’ [The king],
Covering the ears as though [there was] smoking fire [like] a burning nail,
Fearing because of the burning of the chest, trembling, [listen to]
The lack of disgrace for those who were born in the divine clan [of]
The hands that broke the golden crown [at] the top [of the head/heavens]
[And] the bright bangle of the king of the celestials [with] wide hands [with]
The Vacciram weapon! Still listen, [to] the manifesting of the good truth!
In the good land [with] harvests that cannot be sustained [= being too much],
Of the king of the city of Pukār [with] tanks [with] floods of flowers
[The king who] entered [himself as] the weight of a dove, he who brought justice
To the milk-cow, [with] broad hands [with] a perfect spear that produced
A great feast, the seat of great [= auspicious] days that obtained
A brilliant state [of character], a straight sceptre that knows Dharma
[And] a long sword of a brave path, a skilled Brahmin called Parācaraṉ,
Having heard about the power of charity of the Cēraṉ [with] a gleaming spear,
Saying, ‘Let me see the Cēralaṉ [with] a tall spear, with firm power,
[Thus] going [through] forests, lands [and] towns, having gone [far away]
So that the extensive Malayam lands fell behind, completing [an education in]
The four Vedas of the wealth of the three fires, studying the great order
Of the Brahmins of six tasks, who protect the tasks that are done [with]
The five great sacrifices [by] those who are twice-born [with] principles
That desire [only] one thing (?); [thus] gaining skill of the tongue,
Having driven away enemies, wearing the Vākai of the Brahmins,
Gaining acceptance, he would return to his home, carrying a good vessel.
In the town called Taṅkāl of the people of the Vedas, [with] refined work,
Of the southern one [with] a straight sceptre, he who sat, separated,
In the village common [with] a Bodhi tree that was full of green leaves,
With a staff, a pitcher, a white parasol, a ritual stick, a small hoard of things
Along with anklets [on] the feet, saying, ‘May the victorious one of
Ancient victory, [his] guardian white parasol having become plentiful,
Live long! May the guardian who overthrew the Kaṭampu of the sea
Live long! May the king who impressed a bow on the valley [of the Himalayas]
Live long! May Poṟaiyaṉ of beautiful, cool Porunai live long!
May the beautiful Cēraṉ king Māntar live long!’ All the little kids in play
Having left behind [their] playmates of the group, [with]
Curly hair, top-knots, prattling red mouths [and] slackened gaits,
Water tanks! Having recited along with, Leave, having taken [my]
Small hoard of [precious] things!’, the son of a Vaiśya of excellence
Befitting of excellence, was one who was grown, having taken the name
Of the Lord abiding [under] the banyan (Ālamar Celvaṉ), [with]
The excellent one being amazed by [this] Takkiṇaṉ who recited,
So that it was comparable with [his] smile abundant with drops
[= Pearls = teeth], not deviating from the sounds of the Vedas,
Despite [having] a mouth [with] a tongue that slackened [= was immature],
Having given [the child] a gift [of] an ornamental string of pearls, a bracelet
[Which was] a jewel [for] wearing that was befitting of that [string],
Along with earrings, he being one who returned to his own abode
As [the child] put on the good jewels, wearing [them], [the other children]
Becoming those who could not bear [their jealousy], being those who put
Vārttikaṉ [in] the jail-house where [criminals] were deposited, saying
To the young men [of] royal employment, they having protected as guards,
‘He is the Brahmin who seized [= stole] the objects that fell [into his hands]
Meant only for the order of the king!’ She who is called Kārttikai,
The wife of the Vārttikaṉ, she who suffered, having lamented,
Mourned on the ground. She rolled [on the ground]. She rose [again].
Seeing that, because the door of fine handiwork at the temple of Aiyai
Of faultless excellence did not open, the king [with] a brave spear
Being perturbed as one who heard [about] the firm door that was shut
Without opening, [the king] saying, ‘Is there a curved [= unjust] sceptre?
Does anyone know anything about the affliction that affects Koṟṟavai so?’
As young men [under] command, having bowed to the guardian [= the king],
Narrated the truthful story [about] the [slander] that Vārttikaṉ brought,
[The king] speaking long[-lasting] words, saying ‘This is not proper’,
[Also] saying [to Vārttikaṉ], ‘It is [your] duty/[my] debt as the one who bears
The failure of my divine order, which changed [in its] state of [good] order
Because of ignorant men’, having granted [Vārttikaṉ] a paddy field
Along with Taṅkāl itself, [then] having granted [his] brilliant chest
To the lady of the vast earth [= having laid prostrate], in front of
Vārttikaṉ, the husband of Kārttikai, he even cooled down her
Unabating desire, just a little bit. The lady who was seated [on]
The stag [finally] opened the door, so that [the sound] was heard
Everywhere, [in] Kūṭal with stature, [on] the long streets [and]
The storied houses that resembled mountains. [The king], saying,
‘Remove/destroy the buildings in which [people were] jailed. Do
The release [from] tax[-obligations] everywhere, [for] people who pay tax.
Whether it be wealth that was given, or wealth that was chanced upon,
The profit of those who possess is now [also] for those who obtained [it].’
Placing a beautiful Muracu drum on the neck of an elephant,
Listen you, to the appropriate nature in which the victorious king
Who beat [even] the order of [his] sceptre, deviated from his [own] order!
Oh lady [with] rows of bangles, there is a saying that goes,
‘On the eighth day, of the Kuṭam star joining the Aḻal star on the
Great, dark side of the moon of the Āṭi month, on the Veḷḷi day
Of the week, the king, along with Maturai that is worthy of fame
Will experience destruction, as a bright flame consumes [it].’
In the good land of Kaliṅkam, adorned by fragrant groves,
As Vacu and Kumaraṉ [with] broad hands [with] sharp spears -
The rightful kings that were born in an excellent family of
Undestructible brilliance of the kings [with] rows of garlands
Of the prosperity of kings that rule in Ciṅkapuram with sweet floods
[And] tanks, and in Kapilapuram [with] forests from which stalks rise,
Became enemies with each other; throughout [a distance of] six
Kāvatams of land, because of [this] fighting, [this] strong conquest,
Because of an absence of travellers [i.e., traders coming into town],
The trader called Caṅkamaṉ who traded rare jewels, having come to
A bazaar in Ciṅkapuram of praise undiminishing in its strength,
Out of a desire for rare wealth, having carried [along with him]
Great jewels, along with [his] lady-love, like people who live in disguise,
Was your Lord. In a previous life, [he was] the husband of [the lady with]
Green-gold bracelets. Kōvalaṉ, of the name Parataṉ, would carry out
Royal tasks for the king of intense power. Because Kōvalaṉ [of] the names
[Among which was] also Parataṉ was one who was bitter/detested
In which [he] left the vow [of non-violence], having taken the man
As the one who captured [him], saying, ‘This guy is the spy!’,
Showing [him] to the king [with] a victorious spear, when he killed [him],
The wife of Caṅkamaṉ who [also] fell upon the site of the murder,
Could not see a place to stay [anymore]. She who was called Nīli,
Saying, ‘Is this the way of the kings? Or, is it the way of the fishermen?
Or, is this the way of you townspeople? Or, is it the way of the villagers?’
She went to [every] village common, to every street. After having wandered
[For] upto a double of seven days [= fourteen days], saying,
‘This is the day [of me] bowing [to him]’, praising [him] as [he] appeared,
She stood, having united with him who was murdered, upon a ladder
[To] the great skies, having climbed to the peak of a mountain.
Saying, ‘May it be, that everyone who created the grief that has befallen us
[Also] have misery themselves.’ Because you experienced the curse
That had no fault/deviations that was placed by her who fell [to the ground],
Listen, you, to [this] speech: when Karma takes shape, having [already]
Come [in] the previous birth, performing penance is no use for those who
Do not have good character. [Oh lady with] flowing, sprouting tresses!
Having left your united husband [for] upto fourteen days, you will not see
[him] in [any] form of the people here, but only in the form of the celestials’
Thus, the great deity of Maturai having explained the manner of the rules
To the great chaste wife, after the heat took leave, [Kaṇṇaki] saying,
‘Apart from after having seen [my] husband of [my] intentions,
I am one who does not sit. I am one who does not stand.’ Breaking [her]
Golden bracelets [on] the door of [the temple of] Koṟṟavai, saying,
‘[Once] I entered the eastern entrance [to Maturai] with [my] husband.
May I depart as one who is devoid [of him] [through] the western door.’
Being helpless, as one who confused day and night, having reached
One of the shores of Vaiyai [with] strong waters, because she was descending,
Having suffered, she did not speak [out of her] suffering. Because she climbed
Having placed [her feet on] peaks, she did not ask to eat. Having placed
[Her] feet [on] the hill of Neṭuvēḷ (Murukaṉ) [with] a long spear [like]
A glowing leaf which surpassed the Asuras, having torn up the belly of the seas,
[And] having split the chest/heart of the mountain; under the branches of
A blossoming kino tree, [she] having lamenting, saying ‘I am a lady of evil deeds!’
After the limit of a double of seven days (= fourteen days) had gone by,
Saying, ‘This is the day of greeting [him]’, praising [him] as [he] appeared,
The great lady having praised [him with] big names, a shower of great blossoms
That do not wither having rained [upon her], as the king of the immortals himself
Praised, having arrived [at the scene], indeed, she - Kaṇṇaki [with] beautiful
Tresses that the forest desired, climbed onto a celestial vehicle along with Kōvalaṉ,
Whom the royal city had failed.
